category_summary:
**OpenAI** dominated the day with three major moves: **Sam Altman** [unveiled **GPT-5.5-Cyber**](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-d57e313e22de), a specialized frontier cybersecurity model for critical defenders, [announced a major **Codex** upgrade](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-7c9fb376d1c4) expanding beyond coding to general computer tasks, and **Greg Brockman** [revealed **Chronicle**](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-91b449d69118), a passive-memory feature giving Codex awareness of user activity.

- **Andrej Karpathy** [shared a deeply substantive recap](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-875fc0b1f7cd) of his **Sequoia Ascent 2026** fireside chat, outlining how LLMs enable entirely new paradigms rather than just speeding up existing workflows
- **François Chollet** [pushed back on AI job-replacement narratives](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-5132341e12e7), arguing AI automates tasks not jobs and lacks true autonomy
- **Anthropic** [published research analyzing 1M conversations](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-5d61663f7397) to understand sycophancy patterns in **Claude**, drawing significant community attention
- **Ethan Mollick** [raised governance concerns](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-2b4ab4be163a) about **Anthropic's Mythos** model restrictions, questioning who decides cybersecurity risk thresholds
- **NVIDIA** [reported **SGLang** hitting 180 tok/s/GPU](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-e2c17a04c914) on **DeepSeek-V4** decode with ~1M context on **Blackwell**, the first public performance benchmarks for this configuration
- **LlamaIndex** founder [argued filesystems are becoming](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-2e6276082f4e) the default agent abstraction layer — the 'new RAG stack in 2026'
- **levelsio** [went viral demonstrating](/?date=2026-05-01&category=social#item-2557d2327ed3) a vibe-coded **Stripe** dispute responder, winning his first $1,190 chargeback with AI-generated evidence PDFs

items.title:
Fireside chat at Sequoia Ascent 2026 from a ~week ago. Some highlights:

The first theme I tried to ...
items.content:
Fireside chat at Sequoia Ascent 2026 from a ~week ago. Some highlights:

The first theme I tried to push on is that LLMs are about a lot more than just speeding up what existed before (e.g. coding). Three examples of new horizons:

1. menugen: an app that can be fully engulfed by LLMs, with no classical code needed: input an image, output an image and an LLM can natively do the thing.
2. install .md skills instead of install .sh scripts. Why create a complex Software 1.0 bash script for e.g. installing a piece of software if you can write the installation out in words and say "just show this to your LLM".  The LLM is an advanced interpreter of English and can intelligently target installation to your setup, debug everything inline, etc.
3. LLM knowledge bases as an example of something that was *impossible* with classical code because it's computation over unstructured data (knowledge) from arbitrary sources and in arbitrary formats, including simply text articles etc.

I pushed on these because in every new paradigm change, the obvious things are always in the realm of speeding up or somehow improving what existed, but here we have examples of functionality that either suddenly perhaps shouldn't even exist (1,2), or was fundamentally not possible before (3).

The second (ongoing) theme is trying to explain the pattern of jaggedness in LLMs. How it can be true that a single artifact will simultaneously 1) coherently refactor a 100,000-line code base *and* 2) tell you to walk to the car wash to wash your car. I previously wrote about the source of this as having to do with verifiability of a domain, here I expand on this as having to also do with economics because revenue/TAM dictates what the frontier labs choose to package into training data distributions during RL. You're either in the data distribution (on the rails of the RL circuits) and flying or you're off-roading in the jungle with a machete, in relative terms. Still not 100% satisfied with this, but it's an ongoing struggle to build an accurate model of LLM capabilities if you wish to practically take advantage of their power while avoiding their pitfalls, which brings me to...

Last theme is the agent-native economy. The decomposition of products and services into sensors, actuators and logic (split up across all of 1.0/2.0/3.0 computing paradigms), how we can make information maximally legible to LLMs, some words on the quickly emerging agentic engineering and its skill set, related hiring practices, etc., possibly even hints/dreams of fully neural computing handling the vast majority of computation with some help from (classical) CPU coprocessors.

items.title:
AI automates tasks, not jobs, and when a task gets cheaper, demand for the job grows. 

AI cannot au...
items.content:
AI automates tasks, not jobs, and when a task gets cheaper, demand for the job grows. 

AI cannot automate jobs end-to-end because it lacks autonomy and cannot operate without supervision. There is still zero job from 2022 that can be performed end-to-end by AI, not even translator or customer support associate.

items.title: Mythos seems to be a very capable model based on available information, but it is not a cybersecurit...
items.content:
Mythos seems to be a very capable model based on available information, but it is not a cybersecurity model - it is an advanced general purpose model that happens to be good at cyber because it is good at a bunch of things. Anthropic stated that they were worried about cybersecurity risk, and their efforts mean it is a restricted model with lots of government attention.

OpenAI and Google will pass the same threshold soon (and may already have with unreleased models). and the question is whether they are as worried about cybersecurity risks, or whether they think their guardrails will hold. Currently, the degree to which models have cyberrisk is entirely self-reported and not regulated. That means that OpenAI and Google could release Mythos-class models if they want, by assessing the risk differently and making different decisions.

Does that mean Anthropic is at a disadvantage because it can't release its equivalent model? Will OpenAI and Google also be somehow restricted from releasing their Mythos competitor. It all seems pretty unclear right now.
items.author: @emollick
items.summary: Following yesterday's [Research](/?date=2026-04-30&category=research#item-20a531ad7881) on AI risk reporting, Emollick provides detailed analysis of Anthropic's Mythos model and cybersecurity implications - noting it's a general model restricted due to cyber capabilities, and questioning whether OpenAI/Google will face similar restrictions for equivalent models since risk is self-reported
items.reasoning: Highly insightful policy analysis from a credible source about AI safety governance gaps. Raises critical questions about competitive dynamics when safety assessments are self-reported and not regulated

items.title:
This is really well thought out.

Filesystems are the new default abstraction for agents to interact...
items.content:
This is really well thought out.

Filesystems are the new default abstraction for agents to interact with documents (the new RAG stack in 2026). The issue is actually figuring out how to productize this; you can't "productize" Claude Code over a local file system.

Seems like this tool has all the semantics of filesystems with the versioning of git

items.title: “If you want to know whether the AI bubble is bursting, there’s only one publicly traded company tha...
items.content:
“If you want to know whether the AI bubble is bursting, there’s only one publicly traded company that will tell you: Oracle…”

“Oracle has the lowest credit rating. It also has the greatest debt load, even before the infrastructure buildout came into play…”

“OpenAI…  is by far the biggest customer represented in Oracle’s remaining performance obligations (RPOs), which represent how much money Oracle is slated to earn from its existing contracts. Of the $553 billion in RPOs that Oracle reported in its most recent earnings release, more than $300 billion is OpenAI” And OpenAI is facing severe headwinds. 

“What happens to Oracle if OpenAI shits the bed? One possibility is that it sacrifices its software stock premium and gets priced like a utility, … and  fades in significance … It’s not impossible that [Oracle] goes bankrupt”

And of course it’s not impossible that Ellison’s buddy in the White House might make the rest of us bail it out.

Must read piece by @mslopatto @verge lays out why Oracle is is overextended, and why it might augur the deflation of an AI bubble.

https://t.co/CQGtKe9c3Q
items.author: @GaryMarcus

items.title: I worry deeply already about companies controlling access to very powerful AI, which will come in a ...
items.content:
I worry deeply already about companies controlling access to very powerful AI, which will come in a soft form with very expensive subscriptions. 

This is a step further, with the government confusingly exerting control without clear explanation. 

This control of AI can create massive dystopian societies. It’ll rapidly lead to concentration of power. 

Having open models follow closely in capabilities is a great way to minimize political and power games here.

items.title:
Warning: GenAI-induced cognitive surrender may kill innovation.

Consider the following, from CEO @w...
items.content:
Warning: GenAI-induced cognitive surrender may kill innovation.

Consider the following, from CEO @wadhwa’s newsletter:

“I have been speaking with recent graduates in India, many of them highly recommended, and on paper they look exceptional. The emails are polished, the resumes are perfect, the proposals are structured and articulate. To filter for real thinking, I even created a job description that required candidates to answer a series of questions before I would interview them, and I explicitly told them not to use AI.
Of course, almost everything I am getting back is AI slop.

The moment you get into a real conversation, it becomes obvious. They cannot explain what they wrote, cannot walk through their reasoning, and fall back on vague generalities when pushed. After seeing this repeatedly, I have come to a difficult conclusion: AI has already done real damage. It has taken people from a weak education system and given them a powerful crutch, and instead of using it to improve their thinking, many are using it to avoid thinking altogether.

And before my American friends think they are any better, they should know it is no different here.”

items.summary: Gary Marcus shares concerns about 'cognitive surrender' from GenAI - recent graduates producing polished AI-generated work but unable to explain their reasoning or demonstrate real thinking when questioned

We looked at 1M conversations to understand what questions people ask, how Claude responds, and where it slips into sycophancy. We used what we found to improve how we trained Opus 4.7 and Mythos Preview.

items.title: 🏆 For the first time in a decade on @Stripe I've started winning disputes with my vibe coded dispute...
items.content:
🏆 For the first time in a decade on @Stripe I've started winning disputes with my vibe coded dispute responder

I used to ignore disputes so I almost always lost them, now I've started winning, this one is the first big dispute for $1,199 USD!

Whenever a dispute comes in, my site gets a webhook notice from Stripe, it then starts collecting evidence and generates a PDF with entire user's details, when they signed up, and most importantly what they did in the app

In this case the user used the app for months, generated thousands of photos then tried to get the money back from their bank

The evidence has to be REALLY detailed, and REALLY good, which is why it's perfect to vibe code it, you can get quite detailed with different types of users and activity on your app, and put that all in the PDF

I'm shocked because I again I never would win disputes before

People in US especially abuse the [ chargeback ] or [ dispute ] en masse, unlike the rest of the world, it's easily built into their banking app next to every transaction, so it's one tap to get free stuff. And why not? You get free stuff!

It's destructive for business owners like me on many levels, if I get over 1% disputes on my account, I risk getting shutdown permanently by Stripe, Visa and MasterCard, like permanently for life, not just my business but on my personal name too, it's ruthless

Disputes are also super expensive for business owners: you don't just pay back the amount they disputed, for every dispute you pay $30, which you only get back if you win!

But with AI we can now create our own tools to fight back against dispute abuse and finally win! 🎉

items.title: What people call "distillation" is a super common practice (you use other models to benchmark your m...
items.content: What people call "distillation" is a super common practice (you use other models to benchmark your model, to evaluate your inputs or to add a little bit to your datasets) that in my opinion should be covered by fair use (just like using public data is), especially when the resulting models are open-source as it benefits all and helps break out monopolies that are strongly forming in AI!
items.author: @ClementDelangue
items.summary: Clement Delangue argues distillation should be covered by fair use, especially when models are open-source - helps break monopolies forming in AI

this is … odd. 

the whole problem with agentic code is that people wind up with hard to debug redun...
items.content:
this is … odd. 

the whole problem with agentic code is that people wind up with hard to debug redundancies, security holes, deleted databases, etc., which is exactly why you need roadmaps and code reviews, with AI-written code.  

I am with @Grady_Booch on this one (and almost everything else).

I don’t doubt that we will have more code written cheaper than ever, but a lot of it is going to be a mess unless there is serious oversight. (Companies like Amazon have already seen this.)

items.title: I started using the concept in 2016 (e.g. in my NIPS 216 keynote, in which I called it a "world simu...
items.content:
I started using the concept in 2016 (e.g. in my NIPS 216 keynote, in which I called it a "world simulator"). 
I published papers on video prediction in 2016.
This was meant to be a key step to train world models.
Ha&Schmi appeared in 2018.

The slide below is from a talk I gave at Brown in Nov 2017.
Full deck here: https://t.co/gwG1gypq5I
We were hoping to train world models through video prediction.
At the time, we were using generative architectures.
We tried latent-variable models and GAN-style training.
But never quite worked on natural video.
Around 2021, I realized that predicting at the pixel level was not a good idea.
That's when the JEPA concept emerged: find an abstract representation within which predictions are performed.
items.author: @ylecun
items.summary: Yann LeCun traces history of his work on world models - from 2016 'world simulator' concept through video prediction, to realizing pixel-level prediction wasn't working, leading to JEPA concept around 2021 for abstract representation-based prediction
items.reasoning: Important historical context from Meta's chief AI scientist about the evolution of world models research, clarifying intellectual lineage. Low engagement suggests this is deep in a thread, but the content is substantive.
items.summary_html: Yann LeCun traces history of his work on world models - from 2016 'world simulator' concept through video prediction, to realizing pixel-level prediction wasn't working, leading to JEPA concept around 2021 for abstract representation-based prediction
items.content_html:
I started using the concept in 2016 (e.g. in my NIPS 216 keynote, in which I called it a "world simulator").

I published papers on video prediction in 2016.

This was meant to be a key step to train world models.

Ha&Schmi appeared in 2018.

The slide below is from a talk I gave at Brown in Nov 2017.

Full deck here: https://t.co/gwG1gypq5I

We were hoping to train world models through video prediction.

At the time, we were using generative architectures.

We tried latent-variable models and GAN-style training.

But never quite worked on natural video.

Around 2021, I realized that predicting at the pixel level was not a good idea.

That's when the JEPA concept emerged: find an abstract representation within which predictions are performed.

items.title:
I'm spending so much time managing context, and I hate it.

Here is a tip for you: don't use /compac...
items.content:
I'm spending so much time managing context, and I hate it.

Here is a tip for you: don't use /compact any more in Claude Code. There's a much better option.

/compact takes your entire conversation history in memory and compresses it into a summary. This frees up tokens, but you'll lose a ton of important details (sometimes up to 70% of what matters!).

On top of that, the summarized context is still tied to the current session and won't persist beyond it.

Here is what you should do instead:

1. Dump the entire conversation history to a markdown file
2. Call /clear to clear the context
3. Start your next prompt by pointing to the markdown file

There are several advantages to doing this:

1. You don't lose any valuable information
2. You control what's in the file
3. The context persists beyond the current session

In summary, when you hit a context limit, do a *handoff*, not a *cleanup*.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino provides detailed tip: use /clear + markdown file handoff instead of /compact in Claude Code to preserve context
items.reasoning: Highly practical Claude Code workflow tip, strong engagement (327 likes, 45K views), specific actionable advice for developers

items.title:
Google just made AI agents learn from experience like humans do. 

The new framework is called Reaso...
items.content:
Google just made AI agents learn from experience like humans do. 

The new framework is called ReasoningBank.

Instead of retraining models, it gives agents a memory layer. 

Every task run gets distilled into a short strategy card. 

Successes become playbooks. Failures become traps to avoid.

Before each new task, the agent pulls the most relevant cards.

The workflow is dead simple:

> Run the task
> Label the outcome
> Extract the lesson
> Store it in the bank
> Retrieve on next match

Success rates jumped 8.3% on WebArena and 4.6% on SWE-Bench Verified. 

Execution steps dropped by roughly three.

Pair it with memory-aware test-time scaling and the gains compound further.

No labeled data. No fine-tuning runs. 

Same weights, sharper behavior every cycle.

items.content:
The four choices – QK normalization, grouped-query attention, sliding window attention, & shorter pretraining context length – all affect how a model decides which parts of its input to focus on.

Update after 2 months, I think Anthropic succesfully killed OpenClaw

"The real power user for OpenClaw has become my gf who I put it in a group chat with me, her and OpenClaw, she's mostly stopped using ChatGPT etc and now only uses AI over Telegram with OpenClaw, it's much more user friendly for her in Telegram iOS than ChatGPT's own app"

items.title:
How we prompt AI is very different in 2026 than 2022 when ChatGPT came out.

I'm teaching a new cour...
items.content:
How we prompt AI is very different in 2026 than 2022 when ChatGPT came out.

I'm teaching a new course, AI Prompting for Everyone, to help you become an AI power user — whatever your current skill level.

It covers skills that apply across ChatGPT, Gemini, Claude, and other AI tools. How to use deep research mode for well-researched reports on complex questions. How to give AI the right context, including more documents and images than most people realize you can provide. When to ask AI to think hard for several minutes on important decisions like what car to buy, what to study, or what job to take. And how to use AI to generate images, analyze data, and build simple games and websites.

I also cover intuitions about how these models work under the hood, so you know when to trust an answer and when not to.

Along the way, you'll see flying squirrels, a creativity test, some of my old family photos, and fireworks.

Join me at https://t.co/tcQc4iJAJG

items.title: Last week, we made Gemini Embedding 2, our first natively multimodal embedding model, available to t...
items.content:
Last week, we made Gemini Embedding 2, our first natively multimodal embedding model, available to the general public. Since then, developers have used it to build video analysis tools, visual shopping assistants, and more.

But you might be wondering... what is an embedding model? 🤔 Let’s break it down! 

1. What is it?
Think of an embedding model as a "universal translator." It takes text, images, video, and audio data and turns them into a long string of numbers, like a unique digital fingerprint.

2. How does it work?
Historically, search has been text only. Now, instead of just matching data by keyword, Gemini Embedding 2 maps multiple modalities in the same space based on meaning. It "feels" the connection between a video of a soccer goal and the words "game-winning shot" without needing tags.

For example, "ocean" and "waves" are placed close together, but "ocean" and "toaster" are miles apart.

3. How can you use it?
Developers have been using it to incorporate smarter search functionality into their builds. This means creating tools where you can snap a photo of a product and type "find this in yellow," or search through thousands of hours of video by describing what happens in a scene.

4. Ready to try it out for yourself?
You can start using it today via the Gemini API or the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform.

In testing, AI co-clinician matched or outperformed physicians in 68 out of 140 assessed areas, including triage.

Yet humans were easily better at spotting crucial red flags and guiding physical exams - showing how these tools could augment clinical judgment. See the benchmarks ↓

items.title: Our research goal for AI co-clinician is to support medical decision making with high-quality eviden...
items.content:
Our research goal for AI co-clinician is to support medical decision making with high-quality evidence.

We tested the system while adapting the NOHARM safety framework, and found it made zero critical errors in 97 of 98 primary care queries - outperforming comparable systems in blind evaluations.

items.title:
You can now make coding agents 10% smarter without touching the model. 

Coding agents rely on a "ha...
items.content:
You can now make coding agents 10% smarter without touching the model. 

Coding agents rely on a "harness" of prompts, tools, and configurations that shape how they work. 

Tuning this harness is usually manual, slow, and breaks easily.

A new paper introduces Agentic Harness Engineering (AHE), a framework that lets the harness improve itself.

It works through three layers:

> Every component becomes a file you can edit or roll back
> Millions of trajectory tokens get distilled into searchable evidence
> Each edit is paired with a prediction that gets verified next round

Every change becomes a contract you can prove or undo.

After ten iterations on Terminal-Bench 2, pass@1 jumped from 69.7% to 77.0%, beating the leading hand-tuned baseline at 71.9%.

The evolved harness transfers across model families with up to 10.1 point gains. 

It also uses 12% fewer tokens on SWE-bench-verified.
items.author: @AlphaSignalAI
items.summary: AlphaSignal covers new paper on Agentic Harness Engineering (AHE) - framework for self-improving agent harnesses, achieving 10% improvement without model changes
items.reasoning: Significant technical contribution showing agent performance gains through harness optimization rather than model changes; 69.7% to 77.0% on Terminal-Bench 2, transfers across models
items.summary_html: AlphaSignal covers new paper on Agentic Harness Engineering (AHE) - framework for self-improving agent harnesses, achieving 10% improvement without model changes

items.title: We shipped a LlamaParse MCP server to let you parse, classify, split, and generally operate over you...
items.content:
We shipped a LlamaParse MCP server to let you parse, classify, split, and generally operate over your hardest documents with your favorite AI agent 📄🤖

Check out the MCP server: https://t.co/eOeDgzOCI5

This is both a useful feature and a great piece of engineering that we want to share with the community. 💡 MCP does not have built-in file upload support, so we needed to implement a URL-based upload endpoint and couple it with parse operations
💡We built in an integration with @WorkOS OAuth. 
💡We built in observability and rate-limiting 

Huge shoutout to @itsclelia for shipping this! Come check out our blog writeup: https://t.co/XsCZlKnzez

LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.title: It's been about a year ago since I launched a site called 🇵🇹 Only In Portugal to journal the crazy i...
items.content:
It's been about a year ago since I launched a site called 🇵🇹 Only In Portugal to journal the crazy issues we've experienced as foreigners moving to Portugal with both governments agencies and businesses here, most of them quite Kafka-esque in nature

Of course everyone's reaction is "why don't you leave?"

But it is one of the most beautiful countries in the world, which has incredible potential

And it's nicer to fix things, and while complaining about things doesn't make you popular (I've received many death threats), it is oddly effective if you do it in the public sphere:

Collectively complaining about things, as we've seen with Google (they stopped  self-sabotaging and are now the leader in AI), the European Union (they are passing laws based on @euacc points), and even Apple (Tim Cook finally quit), does fix things, eventually!

So I'm trying the same with Portugal

I feel AI governance has a lot of potential, AI can be quite a neutral party that can look at issues and find solutions in a very pragmatic and non-partisan way

So I've asked AI to analyze over 300 issues, stories and experiences submitted to my site in the last 12 months, and write an deep analysis report how to fix Portugal in the next 5 years: every argument it makes is based on real experiences from real people , so no AI hallucinations

AI believes all issues here are based on 5 core problems:
- The Portuguese government is too expensive and too slow to interact with
- There aren't enough skilled workers and no incentive to become one
- There is zero accountability anywhere in the system
- Technology adoption is 15–20 years behind
- The tax system punishes productive people and rewards evasion

(P.S. of course many of Portugal's issues are a microcosm of Europe's macro issues)

AI then created a 5-Year Action Plan to solve it:

YEAR 1 — Shock Therapy
1.1 Flatten the Tax System
1.2 Nuke the Immigration Agency, Build a Digital Replacement
1.3 Gut the Public Sector Bureaucracy 
1.4 The Accountability Law

YEAR 2 — Infrastructure Blitz 
2.1 Lisbon Airport
2.2 Digital Infrastructure
2.3 Healthcare Triage

YEAR 3 — Culture Shift
3.1 Skilled Trades Academy
3.2 Animal Welfare & Noise Enforcement
3.3 Court Reform

YEAR 4 — Economic Acceleration
4.1 Housing
4.2 Transport 
4.3 Consumer Protection

YEAR 5 — Consolidation
5.1 Measure Everything
5.2 Cultural Campaigns
5.3 The Exit Metric

Of course the next challenge is how do you get this to politicians, but we did this with @euacc before, so we can surely do it in Portugal too!

You can read the full action plan in the reply below!

items.title:
The Apple fans are regurgitating a @markgurman report that Apple has given up on Apple Vision Pro.

...
items.content:
The Apple fans are regurgitating a @markgurman report that Apple has given up on Apple Vision Pro.

What am I hearing?

Scuba masks are too hard to sell.

Glasses are the future.

Should I show you my @getVITURE ones to emphasize that point?

Immersion suffers yes. The screens inside my Viture Beast that just started shipping on Monday (under $1,000) have 60-degree field of view (4x bigger than average laptop) where Apple Vision Pro has 90. And there are other VR headsets with far more like what @SomniumSpace makes.

But the smaller, lighter, cheaper form factor is far more acceptable to most consumers. It is way better than watching a movie in the screens in planes.

Me included.

Meta led the way. Its audio and camera Raybans are what people are buying.

Scuba masks are too hard to sell to most consumers.

Glasses with great displays are coming but are also too expensive right now.

So the Holodeck glasses of my dreams will remain niche focused for a few years.

The weight of the Vision Pro is what bugs me.

The Vitures are already getting a lot more use in my life.

Apple knows this.

items.title:
Really good article here about the future of UI in the era of AI:

• We are going in two directions ...
items.content:
Really good article here about the future of UI in the era of AI:

• We are going in two directions right now: headless APIs/CLIs for agents, and generative UIs for humans

• Agents don't click buttons; they call APIs. Applications can't simply force a UI on users and expect them to adapt.

• Most teams are still shipping text-only chat boxes and calling it AI.

• The interface used to ship with the app. Now it ships with the agent, composed at runtime, appearing at the moment of intent, vanishing when done.

• Teams that build the vocabulary their agent speaks will win; teams that settle for long-form text responses with a nice design won't.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Santiago Valdarrama discusses the future of UI in the AI era: headless APIs for agents, generative UIs for humans, interfaces composed at runtime by agents rather than shipped statically with apps.
items.reasoning: Thoughtful synthesis of how AI agents are reshaping UI/UX paradigms. Good engagement (51 likes, 9.5K views). Articulates important emerging design principles: agents call APIs not buttons, interfaces ship with agents not apps.
items.summary_html: Santiago Valdarrama discusses the future of UI in the AI era: headless APIs for agents, generative UIs for humans, interfaces composed at runtime by agents rather than shipped statically with apps.
items.content_html:
Really good article here about the future of UI in the era of AI:

• We are going in two directions right now: headless APIs/CLIs for agents, and generative UIs for humans

• Agents don't click buttons; they call APIs. Applications can't simply force a UI on users and expect them to adapt.

• Most teams are still shipping text-only chat boxes and calling it AI.

• The interface used to ship with the app. Now it ships with the agent, composed at runtime, appearing at the moment of intent, vanishing when done.

• Teams that build the vocabulary their agent speaks will win; teams that settle for long-form text responses with a nice design won't.

items.title: We're launching our first-ever workshop, and we have a lot more planned. If there's a topic you'd lo...
items.content:
We're launching our first-ever workshop, and we have a lot more planned. If there's a topic you'd love us to cover, hit reply.

First up: Harness Engineering. Prompt engineering taught us to write better inputs. Context engineering taught us to structure better information. Harness engineering is what comes next: designing the environments, constraints, and feedback loops that let agents do real work without you in the room.

May 7th, 11am PT. Taught by AJ Joobandi, creator of TechFren and Technical Content Lead at Augment Code. Limited to 20 seats at $150.

Here's what you'll walk away with:

> Why most agents break in production and the environment patterns that fix it
> How to write instruction files, stopping rules, and success metrics for any agent loop
> A framework for designing constraints that let agents iterate without babysitting
> A working harness file you can plug into your own stack today

→ Grab your seat: https://t.co/o3poxpSBHe

items.title: Building a document processing pipeline at scale is hard, and is one of the reasons that it's hard t...
items.content:
Building a document processing pipeline at scale is hard, and is one of the reasons that it's hard to DIY your own document OCR solution by relying on LLM APIs. 

Your orchestration pipeline needs to handle rate-limit issues, handle parsing failure exceptions, handle retries due to timeouts without restarting the whole workflow.

We're excited to collab with @render on this blog post. Get extremely high-quality, scalable document parsing APIs with LlamaParse, and make it even more scalable/resilient in a multi-step workflow through @render's infrastructure! 

Blog: https://t.co/Wd9VMzAfWv
Sample repo: https://t.co/yTsRBfPzHh

LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O
items.author: @jerryjliu0
items.summary: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) promotes collaboration with Render for scalable document processing pipelines using LlamaParse, addressing rate limits, parsing failures, and retries.
items.reasoning: Practical technical content from LlamaIndex founder about production challenges in document processing with LLMs. Addresses real engineering problems (rate limits, failures, retries). Good engagement (89 likes).
items.summary_html: Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex) promotes collaboration with Render for scalable document processing pipelines using LlamaParse, addressing rate limits, parsing failures, and retries.
items.content_html:
Building a document processing pipeline at scale is hard, and is one of the reasons that it's hard to DIY your own document OCR solution by relying on LLM APIs.

Your orchestration pipeline needs to handle rate-limit issues, handle parsing failure exceptions, handle retries due to timeouts without restarting the whole workflow.

We're excited to collab with @render on this blog post. Get extremely high-quality, scalable document parsing APIs with LlamaParse, and make it even more scalable/resilient in a multi-step workflow through @render's infrastructure!

Blog: https://t.co/Wd9VMzAfWv

Sample repo: https://t.co/yTsRBfPzHh

LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.title:
Also waited quite long with this because I was scared it'd be difficult

But I generated a new soldi...
items.content:
Also waited quite long with this because I was scared it'd be difficult

But I generated a new soldier model now in @tripoai (sponsor of the Vibe Jam), and will also try auto-rig it, that gives it a skeleton, so I can modify that and get soldiers to finally walk around instead of statically moving which I have now

I want them to be able to:
- walk
- run 
- crouch
- prone
- hold a gun and point a gun
- jump

And all of these are animations that @ThreeJS then has to run
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio describes using TripoAI to generate a new 3D soldier model with auto-rigging for skeleton/animations in a ThreeJS game, listing desired animations (walk, run, crouch, prone, hold gun, jump).
items.reasoning: Practical demonstration of AI-powered 3D model generation and auto-rigging in game development workflow. Shows real-world application of generative 3D AI tools. Good engagement (91 likes, 26K views).
items.summary_html: Levelsio describes using TripoAI to generate a new 3D soldier model with auto-rigging for skeleton/animations in a ThreeJS game, listing desired animations (walk, run, crouch, prone, hold gun, jump).
items.content_html:
Also waited quite long with this because I was scared it'd be difficult

But I generated a new soldier model now in @tripoai (sponsor of the Vibe Jam), and will also try auto-rig it, that gives it a skeleton, so I can modify that and get soldiers to finally walk around instead of statically moving which I have now

I want them to be able to:

  • walk
  • run
  • crouch
  • prone
  • hold a gun and point a gun
  • jump

And all of these are animations that @ThreeJS then has to run

items.title:
I'm hiring someone to help me build the "Reddit for AI use cases."

People are dying to hear from RE...
items.content:
I'm hiring someone to help me build the "Reddit for AI use cases."

People are dying to hear from REAL people actually doing REAL work with AI.

They don't care about how execs, influencers, or hype chasers are 'using AI'.

A few months ago, we launched our Community Use Cases feature in The Rundown: At the end of every newsletter, we showcase how a reader is using AI to work smarter, save time, or make life easier.

We didn't think much of it, just a cool way to highlight the community.

Well, it became one of the biggest hits the newsletter has ever had.

Now I get daily messages asking us to expand it... so we are.

The plan: build Reddit for AI use cases.

We have the most active and engaged AI-interested audience in the world, with millions of daily readers. Your job is to figure out how they're actually using AI, then incentivize them to share it.

Basically, the friend everyone texts when they hear about a new AI thing... but you're building an entire community of them.

Here's what I'm looking for:

> Someone who thrives in scrappy environments with great communication skills
> Has built online communities at scale before
> Energized by the idea of interviewing thousands of real people about how AI is actually changing their work

This is NOT an easy role. You'll interview thousands of people, gather data points, and dig into their real AI struggles. 

If that sounds fun rather than overwhelming, you're probably who I'm looking for.

Link below to apply!

items.title:
It's crazy to realize how my life depends on AI right now.

I filed my 2025 taxes using AI, read and...
items.content:
It's crazy to realize how my life depends on AI right now.

I filed my 2025 taxes using AI, read and write contracts with it, managed the process of buying, remodeling, and designing an apartment, and have it ingrained pretty much everywhere.

I know these models are unreliable and hallucinate all the time, so I try to be as methodical and careful as possible.

Rule Numero Uno: Never trust a single model.

For anything high-stakes, I compare answers across multiple models.

I use Claude, ChatGPT, Gemini, Gamma 4, and lately Qwen as well.

Sometimes it's annoying to copy and paste across multiple models, but 100% worth it.

I think I found a better process.

The @cueyofficial team reached out to me with a solution for this.

They built a Chrome extension that runs alongside any AI tools you already use.

When you send a prompt, Cuey checks other models in the background and gives you the stronger answer if one is available.

No need to copy/paste across models. Cuey does that behind the scenes.

A few notes:

• It works inside ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini directly. You don't need to change your workflow.

• It supports 30+ models across tiers, from fast/cheap to advanced reasoning models.

• It's positioned specifically for judgment-heavy work: research, strategy, contract review, tradeoff analysis.

• Privacy-first: they don't log your prompts or use them for training.

You'll find the link below.

items.title: @GrumpyTechBro @wholemars Even Bill Gates didn't. I sat next to Tim O'Reilly at the first Google Adv...
items.content:
@GrumpyTechBro @wholemars Even Bill Gates didn't. I sat next to Tim O'Reilly at the first Google Advertisers Conference, and he showed me all of Web 2.0. He had just written the Web 2.0 memo that kicked off the movement. I went home to Microsoft, where I was working at the time, and wrote Bill Gates and the executive team a memo saying they should buy Web 2.0 and laid it all out.

The answer that came back was that my ideas had no business value. Why was that true? Because back then, they were running a big company selling billions of dollars of Office and Windows. They couldn't understand how this stupid little thing that looked like a penny would matter. 

The problem is, if you have a penny that doubles and keeps doubling, eventually it turns into billions of dollars. Eventually, Bill Gates did listen to some of my recommendations; for example, he bought Skype for $8,000,000,000. If he had listened to me earlier, he would have bought it for $2,000,000,000 or less. 

This is what happens: people don't understand exponents. They don't understand that things start small and keep doubling. RoboTaxi is in that exact place right now. It is just going to keep doubling and doubling. The only question is: how fast are the doubles? 

Well, it looks like they are pretty fast:
1. We have the equivalent of 30 pennies on our table right now.
2. In a couple of weeks, it will be 60 pennies.
3. In a few more weeks after that, it will be 120 pennies.

How many doubles do you need to get to millions of dollars? About 25 to 27. So, just hang out and wait for the doubling effect to kick in. Everybody will figure it out around day 23 (or double 23) when the money and the numbers start getting extraordinary. That is coming later this year or next year. 

It doesn't matter. Stay in this for 10 years and you are going to see many millions of RoboTaxis on the road.
items.author: @Scobleizer
items.summary: Scobleizer gives extended analysis of exponential growth applied to Tesla RoboTaxi, drawing parallels to Web 2.0 era when Bill Gates dismissed his recommendations. Claims he wrote a memo to Gates about Web 2.0 while at Microsoft, was ignored, and Gates later overpaid for Skype. Argues RoboTaxi is at the doubling stage and will reach millions in 10 years.
items.reasoning: Substantive original analysis connecting tech history (personal Microsoft/Gates anecdote) to autonomous vehicle growth thesis. Offers specific predictions about RoboTaxi scaling. Low engagement but rich content.

items.title:
Most teams obsess over models.
But the real challenges show up in:
🧠 Memory → agents hoard context a...
items.content:
Most teams obsess over models.
But the real challenges show up in:
🧠 Memory → agents hoard context and lose signal
🔍 Retrieval → bad chunking = bad answers
📊 Evaluation → hardest problem, least prioritized
🧩 Embeddings → behave very differently beyond text
items.author: @mlopscommunity
items.summary: MLOps Community highlighting real production AI challenges: memory management, retrieval/chunking, evaluation, and cross-modal embeddings
items.reasoning: Insightful framing of where real AI production challenges lie - beyond model selection into memory, retrieval, evaluation, and embeddings
items.summary_html: MLOps Community highlighting real production AI challenges: memory management, retrieval/chunking, evaluation, and cross-modal embeddings
items.content_html:
Most teams obsess over models.

But the real challenges show up in:

🧠 Memory → agents hoard context and lose signal

🔍 Retrieval → bad chunking = bad answers

📊 Evaluation → hardest problem, least prioritized

🧩 Embeddings → behave very differently beyond text

items.title:
In the future you have a choice.

Do you engage brain?

or 

Do you cheat?

There will be other choi...
items.content:
In the future you have a choice.

Do you engage brain?

or 

Do you cheat?

There will be other choices too, such as:

Do you go to the casino? 

Or to the library or maker space?

I fear most will make the easiest choice that drives dopamine in their brain. 

But not you. I expect you to take the hard path.

Why? 

You want to be a better human being, that's why. 

Me also.

items.title:
The third lesson is the one every C-level team should pay attention to right now:

In the age of AI,...
items.content:
The third lesson is the one every C-level team should pay attention to right now:

In the age of AI, governance teams cannot act like the police.

Agents, dashboards, and data products are multiplying fast.
If governance slows the business down, the business will route around it.

The real goal is to become a partner to innovation:
→ make trusted data easier to find
→ make safe usage clearer
→ make new data products faster to launch

That is the big takeaway from this video.

Watch the full conversation.

What do you think is the main reason most enterprise data governance efforts stall, strategy, ownership, or trying to scale before the foundation is ready?

items.summary: Van Loon argues AI-era governance teams must become innovation partners, not gatekeepers, making trusted data easier to find and safe usage clearer

items.title:
Most data governance programs do not fail because of bad technology.

They fail because leaders trea...
items.content:
Most data governance programs do not fail because of bad technology.

They fail because leaders treat governance like a software rollout, when it is really an organizational change effort.

In this conversation I explored with Grimme Bogaerts & Martijn Vanhauwaert from Colruyt Group, one line stood out to me:

"It's not about the tooling."

That mindset is why they scaled to 5,500 users.

Here’s what most executives still get wrong, and what actually worked, with insights from @collibra.

#CollibraPartner

items.title:
The second lesson:

Think big, start small, prove it end-to-end.

What worked here was not building ...
items.content:
The second lesson:

Think big, start small, prove it end-to-end.

What worked here was not building half a glossary and hoping people would adopt it.

They built the foundations properly:
→ business glossary
→ lineage
→ roles and ownership
→ an end-to-end setup that worked for one business first

Then they scaled.

That matters because adoption follows credibility.
If the system is incomplete, the business ignores it.
If it works in practice, usage grows naturally.

items.title:
The first lesson is simple, and very counterintuitive:

Do not start with the tool.

Colruyt started...
items.content:
The first lesson is simple, and very counterintuitive:

Do not start with the tool.

Colruyt started with:
→ processes
→ roles
→ dry runs
→ internal alignment

Only after that did they choose technology.

Even more important, they did not just select a platform.
They selected the tool and the partner together, based on who had the strongest change management approach.

That is a much smarter buying motion than most enterprises use.
items.author: @Ronald_vanLoon

items.title:
Anyone know how to generate collission maps with BVH in ThreeJS?

So I did it on client first for my...
items.content:
Anyone know how to generate collission maps with BVH in ThreeJS?

So I did it on client first for my game but then it took like 3 minutes for my models (mostly buildings on the map), way too slow

So then I generated them on the server as .bvh files and then client downloads them but they're huge like 400mb total

AI says BVH gen should take like 1 second so I don't know what I'm doing wrong

Preferrably I want my drone game to be less than 50mb transferred so it loads fast

Also iOS Safari crashes cause too big files now

THANK YOU

Anyone know how to generate collission maps with BVH in ThreeJS?

So I did it on client first for my game but then it took like 3 minutes for my models (mostly buildings on the map), way too slow

So then I generated them on the server as .bvh files and then client downloads them but they're huge like 400mb total

AI says BVH gen should take like 1 second so I don't know what I'm doing wrong

Preferrably I want my drone game to be less than 50mb transferred so it loads fast

Also iOS Safari crashes cause too big files now

THANK YOU

absolutely enraging.

items.title: Innovate with confidence and access proven AI-powered cyber resilience with Commvault: https://t.co/...
items.content:
Innovate with confidence and access proven AI-powered cyber resilience with Commvault: https://t.co/WTMAQjr8WY

Last week at #GoogleCloudNext, @Commvault released these 2 major announcements:

1) #Commvault Extends the Full Breadth of its Next-Gen Cyber Resilience Platform to Google Cloud:
https://t.co/VldJtVePja

2) Clumio Extends its Rapid Recovery & Resilience Capabilities to Google Cloud:
https://t.co/GemtpYOJP5

TL;DR: The currently existing Cyber / Data / Cloud / Identity / Ransomware Resilience functions within Commvault & Clumio are now easily accessible, integrated, and inter-active across all major cloud providers: AWS, Azure, @GoogleCloud.

This means you can use Commvault's platform services as part of your existing billing / credits / services agreement with the cloud provider — not separate billing, no orphaned provider credits, not managing separate accounts with one provider when you call Commvault services, but truly pay-as-you-go all-in-one integration.

This powerfully enables worry-free hybrid (on-prem + cloud) and multi-cloud (AWS+Azure+GCP) resilience operations (ResOps) with Commvault.

Get unified data protection, cleanroom testing, security, governance, AI-driven threat & anomaly detection, and recovery across your enterprise applications & workloads.

These integrated services start roll out this month, with full roll out coming later. Find them in the cloud Marketplace where your workflows & apps live and operate!

Here is a great summary: "Commvault Cloud on Google Cloud protects BigQuery, Google Compute Engine, Google Kubernetes Engine, Cloud SQL and Google Workspace workloads including Gmail and Google Drive. The product automatically finds workloads in Google Cloud and discovered cloud resources are analyzed for protection risk. Reports enable administrators to see protected versus under-protected workloads and it recommends protection policies based on workload classification. A Cloud Threat Scan helps customers search backups for known threats, recover validated clean data, and reduce reinfection risks. The Cloud Air Gap Protect feature enables immutable, backups isolated from production systems to defend against ransomware and insider threats."
SOURCE: https://t.co/dPU9EB2V9v

Start here: https://t.co/WTMAQjr8WY

@ClumioInc #AIInfrastructure #DataProtection #DataResilience #CyberResilience #IdentityResilience #ResOps #AI #AgenticAI #MultiCloud #Clumio #sponsored
